1: The heads of the fathers' clans of the family of Manasseh son of Joseph come before Moses and the leaders. The case concerns the daughters of Zelophehad (raised first in Numbers 27:1-11). Their right to inherit has been established — now a consequence of that ruling surfaces that requires further adjudication.
2: They report: 'YHWH commanded our lord to give the land as inheritance by lot to the Israelites, and our lord was commanded by YHWH to give the inheritance of our brother Zelophehad to his daughters.' The leaders do not dispute the ruling — they accept it — but they see a problem flowing from it.
3: 'But if they marry into another Israelite tribe, their inheritance will be taken from our ancestral inheritance and added to the inheritance of the tribe into which they marry; so it will be taken away from our allotted inheritance.' At the jubilee, inherited land returns to its tribe — but if a woman from one tribe marries into another, her land goes with her marriage and never returns to the original tribe.
4: When the jubilee of the Israelites comes, their inheritance will be added to the inheritance of the tribe into which they have married; so it will be taken from our ancestral tribe's inheritance. Yovel (jubilee) — the fifty-year reset (Lev 25) — would normally return land. But marriage creates a permanent tribal transfer that the jubilee cannot undo. The daughters' inheritance creates a structural tension in the land distribution system.
5: Moses commands by YHWH's word: 'The tribe of Joseph's sons is right.' YHWH vindicates the complaint. The daughters' inheritance is legitimate; the concern about tribal land loss is also legitimate. Both rulings can stand together — a supplemental regulation resolves the tension without overturning either.
6: 'This is what YHWH commands concerning the daughters of Zelophehad: let them marry whom they think best, but they shall marry only within the clan of their father's tribe.' Full freedom of choice within the constraint of tribal endogamy. The daughters are not coerced into a specific marriage — only required to marry within Manasseh. The narrowest restriction that preserves both inheritance rights and tribal integrity.
7: 'So no inheritance of the Israelites shall transfer from tribe to tribe; for all Israelites shall retain the inheritance of their ancestral tribe.' The principle behind the ruling: tribal land allocations are permanent. The jubilee system (Lev 25) and this endogamy requirement together create a land distribution that resists alienation — God's gift to each tribe stays with each tribe.
8: 'Every daughter who possesses an inheritance in any tribe of the Israelites shall marry one from the clan of her father's tribe, so that all Israelites may retain their ancestral inheritance.' The ruling generalizes beyond Zelophehad's daughters — it becomes standing law for any woman who inherits. The case becomes precedent.
9: 'No inheritance shall transfer from one tribe to another; each of the tribes of Israel shall retain its own inheritance.' The repetition reinforces the principle's permanence and centrality. Land, inheritance, tribe, and identity are interlocked in the Mosaic vision of the land. This is not mere property law — it is a vision of a nation where every family has a stake in the land, a stake that can be recovered and cannot be permanently lost.
10: The daughters of Zelophehad did as YHWH commanded Moses. The text names their obedience before recounting it — their compliance is the point. Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Noah are named individuals, not anonymous women subsumed into a ruling. Scripture preserves their names.
11: Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Noah married sons of their father's brothers — within the clans of Manasseh. Five women, each making the same choice: to obey and marry within their tribe. Their obedience is collective and complete. The jubilee question is resolved not by coercion but by faithful compliance.
12: Their inheritance remained in the tribe of their father's clan. The outcome of their obedience is stated simply: the inheritance stayed. The purpose of the ruling is achieved. The land of Zelophehad — fought for by his daughters (Numbers 27), now preserved by their marriages — remains within Manasseh.
13: 'These are the commandments and the regulations which YHWH commanded through Moses to the Israelites in the plains of Moab by the Jordan at Jericho.' The closing verse gathers all of Numbers 28–36 under one summary and anchors the entire book at a specific place and moment: the plains of Moab, east of the Jordan, facing Jericho. Israel stands at the threshold. The book of Numbers ends not with an arrival but with a readiness — the people poised to enter what all these laws have been preparing them for.
